summary: Gruber's comprehensive review of Apple Vision Pro frames the device as three products in one: a VR/AR headset, a spatial computing platform, and a personal entertainment device. He acknowledges the hardware's significant limitations — it's too heavy, too big, requires an external battery, and costs too much — but argues these are typical first-generation compromises that will improve. He's most impressed by VisionOS's spatial interaction model (look and tap), which he compares to the Mac's point-and-click and iPhone's multitouch as a breakthrough in interaction design. The entertainment experience, particularly watching movies in the TV app's theater mode, is the review's emotional peak. He concludes that Vision Pro is a landmark product on par with the 1984 Macintosh and 2007 iPhone, despite its current limitations.
key_points: Vision Pro's hardware has undeniable first-gen limitations — external battery, excessive weight (650g), large size — but these mirror the compromises of the original Mac and iPhone
VisionOS's 'look and tap' interaction model is a breakthrough comparable to the Mac's point-and-click and iPhone's multitouch, removing a layer of spatial abstraction from computing
The spatial workspace is genuinely expansive and intuitive, with windows anchored in physical space and natural use of depth, offering a productivity canvas no monitor setup can match
Personas for video calls are deep in the uncanny valley — Gruber's wife told him 'Don't ever call me like that again' — but Apple had no choice but to ship the feature
The entertainment experience is the strongest selling point: watching movies in the TV app's theater mode is so compelling it makes a 77-inch OLED TV feel inadequate
money_quotes: Vision Pro isn't even in stores yet and it's already subject to mockery. But, like that original iPhone and the original Macintosh before it, this first Vision Pro is no joke.
Vision Pro's displays are amazing, yet also obviously not good enough.
The Mac gave us 'point and click'. The iPhone gave us 'tap and slide'. Vision Pro gives us 'look and tap'.
I FaceTimed my wife after capturing mine, and her reaction — not really knowing at all what to expect — was 'No, no, no — oh my god what is this?' And then she just started laughing.
There are 3D immersive experiences in Vision Pro that are more compelling than Disney World attractions that people wait in line for hours to see.
In a few years, after a few product generations, we will all look back at this first Vision device and laugh.
VisionOS is, to my eyes, the best-looking OS Apple has made since the original skeuomorphic iPhone interface in iOS 1–6.
Try that, and no matter how skeptical you were beforehand about the Vision Pro's price tag, your hand will start inching toward your wallet.
key_insight: Vision Pro's first-generation hardware is severely limited by today's technology, but VisionOS's spatial interaction model is a genuine breakthrough in computing that will define how all future headsets work, just as the Mac defined desktops and the iPhone defined phones.
